course_info.title: Network Security
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

lecture_notes.introduction: Network security architecture defines how an organization protects its digital assets at the network layer. Modern approaches are moving beyond perimeter-based models toward zero trust.
key_concepts: Network segmentation with VLANs and subnets
Zero Trust Architecture: never trust, always verify
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing and implementing a segmented network with pfSense firewalls, VLANs, and zone-based policies
setup_requirements: pfSense VM or GNS3 with firewall images

review.value: No implicit trust based on network location. Every access request is authenticated, authorized, and encrypted regardless of source. Policy enforcement at every point.
Applying firewall policies at the individual workload level rather than at the network perimeter, limiting lateral movement after a breach

learning_objectives: Configure next-generation firewall rules with application awareness
Implement stateful inspection and deep packet inspection
Design firewall rule sets following the principle of least privilege

lecture_notes.introduction: Next-generation firewalls go beyond simple port and IP filtering to inspect application-layer traffic, providing granular control over what enters and leaves the network.
key_concepts: Stateful inspection vs deep packet inspection
Next-gen features: SSL inspection, threat intelligence feeds
lecture_notes.demonstration: Configuring pfSense with Snort integration for deep packet inspection, writing rules that block specific application behaviors

review.value: Examines the payload of packets, not just headers. Can detect malware, application misuse, and data exfiltration hidden in allowed protocols.
Firewalls evaluate rules top-to-bottom. Most specific deny rules first, then allow rules, with an implicit deny-all at the end. Order affects both security and performance.

learning_objectives: Configure Snort/Suricata IDS rules for threat detection
Differentiate between signature-based and anomaly-based detection
Tune IDS to reduce false positives while maintaining detection

lecture_notes.introduction: IDS and IPS systems are the network's immune system, detecting and responding to threats that bypass perimeter controls.
key_concepts: Signature-based detection: pattern matching against known threats
Snort rule syntax: header (action, protocol, src, dst) and options
IDS placement: inline (IPS) vs passive (IDS) deployment
False positive tuning and threshold configuration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing custom Snort rules to detect a simulated attack, then tuning thresholds to reduce false positives from legitimate traffic
setup_requirements: Snort 3 or Suricata installed on Linux VM
Lab traffic with embedded attack samples

review.value: alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET 80 (msg:'SQL Injection Attempt'; content:'UNION SELECT'; nocase; sid:1000001;)
Suppress rules by source, adjust detection thresholds, whitelist known-good traffic patterns. Goal: high detection rate with actionable alerts.

class_schedule.topic: VPN Technologies and Secure Remote Access
learning_objectives: Configure IPsec site-to-site and remote access VPNs
Compare IPsec with WireGuard and OpenVPN
Implement TLS-based VPN for remote workers

lecture_notes.introduction: VPNs extend secure network access across untrusted networks. The choice of VPN technology impacts security, performance, and manageability.
key_concepts: IPsec: IKE phase 1 and phase 2, AH vs ESP
Split tunneling risks and policy considerations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Setting up a WireGuard VPN between two lab networks and verifying encrypted traffic with tcpdump
setup_requirements: Two Linux VMs as VPN endpoints

review.value: Encapsulating Security Payload provides confidentiality, integrity, and authentication. In tunnel mode, the entire original IP packet is encrypted and encapsulated.
Modern VPN protocol with ~4000 lines of code (vs 100K+ for OpenVPN). Uses Noise protocol framework, ChaCha20, Poly1305, Curve25519. Stateless design with cryptokey routing.

learning_objectives: Deploy network monitoring with NetFlow and packet capture
Detect network anomalies and lateral movement
Build network baselines for behavioral analysis
readings: The Practice of Network Security Monitoring Ch. 3-5

lecture_notes.introduction: You cannot defend what you cannot see. Network monitoring provides the visibility required to detect threats that evade preventive controls.
key_concepts: Full packet capture vs metadata-only approaches
Network behavioral baselines and deviation detection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Using Zeek to analyze pcap files, generating connection logs, and identifying suspicious DNS queries indicative of C2 communication
setup_requirements: Sample pcap files with embedded threats

review.value: Network analysis framework that produces structured logs (conn.log, dns.log, http.log) from traffic. Unlike IDS, it focuses on network visibility and metadata extraction.
Malware often uses DNS for command and control via long subdomain queries, high query frequency, or TXT record exfiltration. Monitoring DNS analytically reveals these patterns.

class_schedule.topic: Network Attack Defense: DDoS, MitM, and DNS Attacks
learning_objectives: Deploy DNS security measures including DNSSEC and DNS-over-HTTPS

lecture_notes.introduction: Network attacks target the availability and integrity of communication channels. Defending against them requires both technical controls and architectural planning.
key_concepts: DDoS mitigation: rate limiting, scrubbing centers, Anycast
ARP spoofing detection with Dynamic ARP Inspection
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulating an ARP spoofing attack with Ettercap, detecting it with arpwatch, and preventing it with Dynamic ARP Inspection on a managed switch

review.value: Volumetric attacks require upstream filtering (ISP scrubbing, CDN absorption). Application-layer attacks require WAF rules and rate limiting. No single solution handles all types.
Port-based NAC using EAP authentication. Unauthenticated devices are placed in a quarantine VLAN. Prevents rogue devices from accessing the network.

questions.question: Design a network architecture for a small company with a public web server, internal corporate network, and guest Wi-Fi. Specify where you would place firewalls, which zones you would create, and write two specific firewall rules (in pseudocode) that enforce the security policy.
questions.answer: Three zones: DMZ (web server), Internal (corporate), Guest. Two firewalls: external (between internet and DMZ) and internal (between DMZ and Internal/Guest). Rules: (1) ALLOW tcp from ANY to DMZ_WEBSERVER port 443 - permits HTTPS to the public web server. (2) DENY all from GUEST_ZONE to INTERNAL_ZONE - prevents guest devices from accessing corporate resources. Additional: allow DMZ to Internal on specific database port only, deny all DMZ-initiated connections to Internal otherwise.

rubric.key: Two valid firewall rules with justification

questions.question: Write a Snort rule that detects potential SSH brute force attacks (more than 5 failed connection attempts within 60 seconds from the same source IP to port 22). Explain each component of the rule.
questions.answer: alert tcp $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET 22 (msg:"SSH Brute Force Attempt"; flow:to_server,established; content:"SSH-"; detection_filter:track by_src, count 5, seconds 60; sid:1000002; rev:1;) Explanation: alert generates notification, tcp specifies protocol, $EXTERNAL_NET any -> $HOME_NET 22 matches external IPs to internal port 22, flow:to_server,established matches established connections to the server, content:"SSH-" matches SSH protocol banner, detection_filter triggers after 5 matches from same source within 60 seconds.

rubric.key: Appropriate use of detection_filter for rate-based detection

quizzes.topic: VPNs, Network Monitoring, and Attack Defense

questions.question: Compare IPsec tunnel mode with WireGuard for a site-to-site VPN connecting two offices. Address: cryptographic primitives used, configuration complexity, performance characteristics, and a specific scenario where each would be preferred.
questions.answer: IPsec tunnel mode uses IKEv2 for key exchange with configurable cipher suites (AES-GCM, SHA-2, DH groups). Configuration is complex with multiple phases and parameters. Performance depends on hardware offload support. WireGuard uses fixed modern primitives (ChaCha20, Poly1305, Curve25519, BLAKE2s). Minimal configuration (public/private keys and allowed IPs). Higher throughput due to simpler code path. Prefer IPsec when regulatory compliance requires specific cipher suites or when integrating with legacy equipment. Prefer WireGuard for modern environments prioritizing simplicity, performance, and auditability.

questions.question: An analyst notices a DNS query log showing hundreds of requests to subdomains like 'a3f2b1c4.d5e6f7.malware.example.com' from an internal workstation. What type of attack does this indicate, how does it work, and what network security controls would detect and prevent it?
questions.answer: This indicates DNS tunneling for data exfiltration or C2 communication. The attacker encodes stolen data or commands in subdomain labels of DNS queries to an attacker-controlled authoritative nameserver, bypassing firewalls that allow DNS traffic. Detection: DNS analytics monitoring for high entropy in subdomain names, unusual query volume per domain, and abnormal TXT record sizes. Prevention: (1) Force all DNS through an internal resolver that blocks known malicious domains. (2) Implement DNS-over-HTTPS inspection on the resolver. (3) Deploy DNS firewall (RPZ) blocking newly registered and uncategorized domains. (4) Set maximum subdomain length and query rate limits.

major_assignments.title: Secure Network Design and Implementation
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a complete secure network infrastructure for a small business using virtual lab equipment. Include segmentation, firewalls, IDS, VPN, and monitoring.
objectives: Implement firewall rules, IDS, and VPN in a virtual environment
Deploy network monitoring and verify detection capabilities
Document the architecture with security justifications
deliverables: Network architecture diagram with security zones and traffic flows
Working virtual lab implementation with all components configured
Firewall rule set documentation with security justification
IDS testing results showing detection of simulated attacks

criteria: Proper zone segmentation with security justification
Zero trust principles applied where appropriate
IDS rules detect test attacks without excessive false positives
Network monitoring deployed and capturing traffic
Security policy for each zone transition
Fully functional implementation with all security controls operational
Verified detection and prevention capabilities through testing
Comprehensive documentation suitable for a real deployment

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of network security concepts and defense techniques

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on labs with firewalls, IDS, VPN, and monitoring tools

major_assignments.description: Comprehensive secure network design and implementation project

midterm_examination.description: Practical exam: configure and defend a network against simulated attacks

final_project.description: Advanced network security architecture for a complex scenario

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in course failure.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs. Design documents and reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All network configurations must be your own work. Sharing firewall rules or IDS configurations between students is prohibited.
course_policies.lab_safety: All network testing must be conducted within the isolated lab environment. Scanning or attacking production networks will result in immediate course failure.
